data.title: North American Morning Briefing: Futures Slip as Markets Fret About Iran Peace Talks -2-
data.description:
Foreign-based automakers have warned the Trump administration that they are looking at pulling their cheapest car models out of the U.S. market if the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement isn't renewed or is watered down, according to people familiar with the discussions.

Companies such as Nissan, Hyundai and Toyota are among the few carmakers to offer U.S. consumers new models of small, affordable automobiles after Detroit-based automakers ditched such cars in recent years in favor of SUVs and trucks.

Write to priscila.barrera@wsj.com

data.title: North American Morning Briefing: Futures Slip as Markets Fret About Iran Peace Talks
data.description:
OPENING CALL

Stock futures were mostly down on Tuesday after the U.S. expressed skepticism about a proposal from Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Investors are worried it will be tough for the market to extend its record-breaking run if there is no further progress in peace talks.

Oil prices climbed above $110 a barrel. With six-month Brent crude futures now up almost 2%, "it's clear that market expectations for lower oil prices ahead are also fading," Deutsche Bank said.

Investors are also awaiting the Federal Reserve's upcoming meeting, where policy rates are expected to stay on hold.

"Markets are less focused on FOMC actions and more on the signal: is elevated inflation a transitory energy story or something more persistent?" First Citizens Wealth said.

DZ Bank expects a virtually unanimous decision to keep rates on hold at Wednesday's rate decision, but as usual, "it remains entirely possible that the ultra-dovish Stephen Miran could once again break ranks and advocate for a 25-basis-point rate cut."

Indosuez Wealth Management continues to believe that the Fed is well-positioned to wait and assess the actual impact of the Middle East conflict, cross asset strategist Lucas Meric says in a note. " Inflation remains persistent, independently of recent events in the Middle East."

Earnings from a host of blue chips are expected later Tuesday, including Coca-Cola, General Motors and Starbucks.

Stocks to Watch

BP earnings more than doubled in the first quarter, buoyed by its oil-trading unit. London-traded shares rose 3%.

Barclays fell 2% after its earnings report included one-off charges tied to the collapse of mortgage lender Market Financial Solutions and a planned regulatory redress scheme for allegedly missold car loans.

Contemporary Amperex Technology said it would raise $5 billion in a share placement. The sale will be at a discount to its closing price Monday. Its stock slid nearly 7%.

Oracle slid nearly 5% premarket after The Wall Street Journal reported that OpenAI--which has a data-center deal with Oracle worth $300 billion-- missed revenue and user targets .

CoreWeave, another OpenAI infrastructure provider, and SoftBank Group, a major OpenAI investor, also tumbled.

Watch For:

U.S. Conference Bd - Consumer Confidence for April, U.S. S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Home Px Index for February, Coca-Cola 1Q Earnings, General Motors 1Q Earnings, Mondelez 1Q Earnings, Starbucks 2Q Earnings, UPS 1Q Earnings, UnitedHealth Group1Q Earnings, Verizon 2Q Earnings, Visa 2Q Earnings

Today's Top Headlines/Must Reads:

- Foreign Carmakers Threaten to Pull Cheapest Models From U.S. Without Trade Deal

- Claudia Sheinbaum Is Learning the Price of Appeasing Trump

- A Bill Aimed at Creating Homes Is Leaving Plots Empty Instead

MARKET WRAPS

Forex:

The dollar rose after Trump sounded notes about Iran not dealing in good faith or being willing to meet his key demand of ending nuclear enrichment and vowing to never make a nuclear weapon, the WSJ reports.

The euro fell against the dollar but could strengthen over the medium term if the Fed cuts rates while the European Central Bank hikes.

Bonds:

Treasury yields rose as oil prices increased. Treasury investors are focused on the Fed's coming meeting , where policy rates are expected to stay on hold.

Discomfort from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is "slowly amplifying," as the 10-year U.S. breakeven inflation rate is edging towards 2.5% , ING said.

"Far from catastrophic, but if it were to break above, it would be a growing problem, for both Treasuries and the Federal Reserve."

The damage to government bond markets from the war in the Middle East could prove longer-lasting than that to equities, Capital Economics said.

U.S. high-yield corporate bonds have proved resilient, despite escalating volatility from the Mideast conflict, artificial-intelligence-related risks for software businesses and concerns about private credit, Nuveen said.

Energy:

In early European trading, Brent crude for June delivery rose 2.9%, while WTI futures for June were up 2.4%. Oil prices may rise further with no immediate signs of the Strait of Hormuz reopening, ING said.

Metals:

Gold prices fell below $4,700 a troy ounce as higher energy prices fuel inflation concerns and reinforce expectations of a higher interest-rate environment.

Nickel

Nickel rose in early Asian trade. Sentiment is supported by the prospect of tighter supplies from Indonesia, as the country has lowered its mining quotas to boost prices and tackle oversupply in the mining industry, ANZ Research said.

Iron

Iron ore prices were lower in early Asian trade amid expectations of weak demand. As China prioritizes initiatives to curb excess competition and production, there is unlikely to be large-scale stimulus for steel in the near term, Nanhua Futures said. 

   TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

There's a Revolution in Cancer. But Can Big Pharma Afford It?

There may soon be a drug for pancreatic cancer. For patients who have faced one of medicine's most merciless diagnoses, it is a genuine breakthrough.

For Wall Street, it raises a different question entirely: Can anyone afford to acquire the drug's developer?

Can Social-Media Fame Power an IPO? Bill Ackman Is About to Find Out.

Bill Ackman wants to capitalize on his social-media fame by opening his hedge-fund strategy to the masses. It remains to be seen how enthusiastic the masses are.

The billionaire investor plans to stage a unique double listing Wednesday, taking public both a new stock-picking fund, Pershing Square USA, and his hedge-fund management company, Pershing Square. To attract investors, he is offering free shares of Pershing Square to anyone who buys into Pershing Square USA.

OpenAI Misses Key Revenue, User Targets in High-Stakes Sprint Toward IPO

OpenAI recently missed its own targets for new users and revenue, stumbles that have raised concern among some company leaders about whether it will be able to support its massive spending on data centers.

Chief Financial Officer Sarah Friar has told other company leaders that she is worried the company might not be able to pay for future computing contracts if revenue doesn't grow fast enough, according to people familiar with the matter.

Meta Is Preparing to Have to Undo Its Manus Acquisition After China Ban

Meta Platforms is preparing to have to unwind its acquisition of the artificial-intelligence startup Manus after China banned the transaction on national-security grounds Monday, according to people familiar with the matter.

Meta bought Manus, a China-linked, Singapore-based startup that specializes in building AI agents, in December for $2.5 billion, and quickly moved to integrate the new technology into its systems. Any attempt to undo the acquisition would mean disentangling the two.

Oil Back Above $111 as Peace Talks Remain Stalled

Oil prices rose above $111 a barrel, dragging on investor sentiment as peace talks between the U.S. and Iran showed no signs of resuming.

Markets traded nervously after the U.S. expressed skepticism in response to an offer from Iran that would open the Strait of Hormuz while postponing talks on Tehran's nuclear capabilities, The Wall Street Journal reported.

The Fed's Rate Cuts Are On Pause. But Are They Also Off the Table?

Two years ago, with the economy chugging along and inflation falling, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell dispatched a question about stagflation with humor rather than scorn. "I don't see the 'stag' or the 'flation,' actually," he deadpanned.

The term-for an economy stuck with both weak growth and high inflation-resurfaced last year when tariffs threatened to lift prices and crimp hiring. But it was still mostly theoretical, a policy choice that could be reversed.

Iran Offers a New Proposal to End War

Iran has presented regional mediators with a new offer to stop its attacks in the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for a full end to the war and a lifting of the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, said officials familiar with the matter.

The proposal, presented by Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi over the weekend, is designed to break the deadlock in the conflict and set talks back in motion, the people said. It would see discussions about Iran's nuclear program shelved. Iran's mission to the United Nations didn't respond to a request for comment.

Fed News and Big Tech Earnings Collide This Week. The Mag 7 Matter Most.

Wall Street is getting ready for one crazy Wednesday: A Federal Reserve meeting, a Senate committee vote on a new Fed chairman, and earnings reports from four Mag Seven companies after the closing bell.

Even if the Fed doesn't do anything to make investors nervous, the market needs Big Tech earnings to live up to the hype. The April market rebound needs more good news if it's going to have room to run.

Suspect Accused Of Trying To Kill President

WASHINGTON-The man accused of opening fire outside a Washington press gala over the weekend was charged on Monday with attempting to assassinate President Trump, as officials defended the security at the high-profile dinner but promised to re-evaluate protocols for future events.

Cole Allen, 31 years old, was also charged with two firearms offenses after officials said he charged past a security perimeter and opened fire at the Washington Hilton, the site of the annual White House Correspondents' Association Dinner. Allen faces a potential life sentence on the attempted assassination charge alone.

Foreign Carmakers Threaten to Pull Cheapest Models From U.S. Without Trade Deal

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

April 28, 2026 06:16 ET (10:16 GMT)

data.title: 'We believe it's real': My mom received a check from a class-action lawsuit. It bounced. Could this be a scam?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'Now they are deducting that amount from her account'

"I handle my elderly mother's finances." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I handle my elderly mother's finances. She got a check in the mail for a class-action settlement and cashed it. It later bounced and now they are deducting that amount from her account. I believe this is a legitimate case of timeshare overcharging. We believe it's real. The bank photocopied the check so I read it. But mom has been advised to not cash any checks moving forward.

What can be done about reaching the settlement people to get this reissued?

The Son

Related: My second husband will leave me $540,000 if I bequeath him my $130,000 net worth. What will happen to my two sons?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

If this was a legitimate check from a law firm giving your mother money for a class-action lawsuit, it would not have bounced.

Dear Son,

Are you sure this class-action check is legitimate?

Verify that before you do anything. You can do so by identifying the official class-action settlement administrator from the photocopy of the check. Do not call the phone number on the check, as it could be fake. Instead, look online to find the correct phone number. Contact the administrator and explain that the check was deposited but later returned unpaid.

If this was a legitimate check from a law firm giving your mother money for a class-action lawsuit, I suspect it would not have bounced. Your mother's bank would not want her to replenish the money they used to cover the cost of this check before it bounced. Even if your mother owns a timeshare, it does not make it real. It means the scammer got lucky.

Fake-check scams are often printed with the names and addresses and logos of legitimate companies and banks. "These scams work because fake checks generally look just like real checks, even to bank employees," the Federal Trade Commission says. They may even be real checks belonging to someone whose identity has been stolen.

Here's how such a scam might work: Your mother cashes the check for, say, $1,000. The money lands in her bank account. The check bounces after five days, and your mother is left on the hook. Your mother tries to find the company that mailed her the check, but it either doesn't exist or the company listed on the check says they never sent it.

Your mother cashes the check for, say, $1,000. It bounces after five days and your mother is left on the hook.

The FTC says it can actually take weeks before the bank discovers a fake check - but even if it takes days, it will usually be too late. A check can take anywhere from one to seven business days or more to clear. "By law, banks have to make deposited funds available quickly," the FTC says. "Even if you see the funds in your account, that doesn't mean it's a good check."

Who doesn't like "free" money? Millions of Americans have trouble making ends meet, and a class-action lawsuit check can feel like awfully good timing. People also fall for "truth bias" (who would go to so much trouble to create a fake check?) and "optimism bias" (people are essentially good, and good things happen to good people).

An "official bias" can also be persuasive. Letters, checks, emails and phone calls from supposedly authoritative organizations - banks, police, universities, Uncle Sam - can be intimidating and, if they're offering you something for nothing, impressive. We are more likely to comply with the request if there is a power imbalance and we feel wrong-footed.

Don't miss: 'I hope to retire early': I'm 56 and have 80% in a traditional IRA and 20% in a Roth. Am I in trouble?

Verifying an unknown check

"Bounced" is an umbrella term. The issuing bank may have rejected it due to a technical or administrative issue. The settlement administrator could have voided or stopped payment on the check. The best immediate action: Ask the bank for answers. They may be able to say there were "insufficient funds" or "suspected fraud" rather than give you a conclusive answer.

The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation says there are several ways to help ensure that a check received in the mail is real. Receiving a check in the mail from an unknown source is the first big red flag. But let's assume that your mother was involved with an actual timeshare and there was an ongoing class-action lawsuit. She may be more likely to deposit the check.

A fake bank name is an obvious warning. "Check with the bank that supposedly issued the check to make sure it is real," the FDIC says. "Make sure you look up the phone number on the bank's official website, and don't use the phone number printed on the check that could be a phone number controlled and answered by the scam artist."

The settlement administrator could have voided or stopped payment on the check. Ask the bank for answers.

The FDIC advises consumers to call the bank to verify the check. "They will likely need to know the check number, issuance date and amount," it says. "Consider how and why you received the check. If someone you don't know initiated the payment, be skeptical and proceed cautiously. Scammers often communicate with their victims via email or text."

"Look where the check was mailed from," the FDIC adds. "If the postmark is not the same as the city and state of the 'supposed' issuing bank, it might be an indication the check is fake. Be especially cautious if it was mailed from overseas. Official checks usually contain watermarks, security threads, color-changing ink and other security features."

Call it the "congratulations! scam." The class-action lawsuit story is similar to the sweepstakes and lottery check scam: You are told you have won and/or been awarded money, and you can deposit this check in your bank account. If this is fake, report it to the Federal Trade Commission, the U.S. Postal Inspection Service and/or your state attorney general.

What's revealing about your mother's check is that two different generations believe it to be real. Seniors are targeted by scammers, if only because they're more likely to be at home if they're retired and perhaps are not as well versed in technology as younger generations. But the Pew Research Center reports that scams are common among all age groups.

If it is a scam, you wouldn't be the first ones fooled by a fake check.

Related: 'It's an expensive piece of equipment': My neighbor asked to borrow my snowblower. Do I say yes?

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I feel overwhelmed': I'm 56 and only have $60,000 in my IRA. Is it too late for me?

'We're aiming for a monthly income of $11,500': I'm 64. I've $1.5 million in a 401(k). How do I time my withdrawals?

'I worked very hard': I'm 71 and have $6 million after scrimping and saving. My son, 33, wants money for a house. Do I say yes?

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-28-26 0601ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Gilmore Bell Adds New Attorney in Kansas City
data.description:
Gilmore Bell Adds New Attorney in Kansas City

Gilmore & Bell, P.C., a leading public finance law firm, is pleased to announce that Stephany Rohleder has joined the firm as an associate in the Kansas City office.

Ms. Rohleder was admitted to the Kansas Bar in 2022 and the Missouri Bar in 2023. She received her B.A. in Political Science (magna cum laude) from Fort Hays State University in 2016 and her J.D. from the University of Kansas in 2022, where she was a Staff Editor of the Kansas Law Review. Ms. Rohleder is a member of the Kansas and Missouri Bar Associations, the Kansas City Metropolitan Bar Association, the Johnson County Bar Association, the Association of Women Lawyers of Greater Kansas City and CREW Kansas City.

About Gilmore Bell

Gilmore Bell is one of the leading public finance law firms in the United States. The firm primarily represents governmental entities and 501c3 organizations to negotiate financing alternatives including bond and lease transactions, economic development incentives and public-private partnerships. For more than 30 years, Gilmore Bell has ranked in the top 10 nationally in the number of bond counsel opinions rendered on municipal bond issues. Gilmore Bell has offices in Missouri, Kansas, Nebraska, Utah and Illinois. For more information, visit us at www.gilmorebell.com.

Media Contact:
Christina Comiskey
info@gilmorebell.com
816-221-1000

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260428299234/en/

data.title: Atlas Copco Says Geopolitical Environment Creates Customer Hesitancy
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping 




  STOCKHOLM--Atlas Copco said it expects near-term customer activity to remain at the current level, as the current geopolitical environment continues to raise concerns and create a level of hesitancy among customers. 



  "The current situation in the Middle East has had limited direct financial effect on the group," said Chief Executive Vagner Rego. "However, given the uncertainties, it is very difficult to predict potential indirect effects." 



  The Swedish industrial tool maker said activity levels were high among customers across the group the first quarter, with overall demand improving compared with the previous year. 



  Order intake for vacuum equipment increased significantly from a year earlier, driven by higher demand from the semiconductor industry, while order intake for industrial compressors was essentially unchanged and volumes for gas and process compressors decreased notably, it said. 



  Industrial assembly equipment and vision solutions orders decreased, while industrial powers tools and power equipment orders increased. 



  It reported group net profit of 6.26 billion Swedish kronor, equivalent to $678.9 million, down from 6.6 billion kronor a year earlier, as revenue dropped 5.1% to 40.54 billion kronor. 



  Analysts polled by FactSet had expected net profit of 6.41 billion kronor on revenue of 41.29 billion kronor. 



  The company received orders worth 45.4 billion kronor in the quarter, against a FactSet forecast of 46.48 billion kronor. 





  Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 28, 2026 05:52 ET (09:52 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Barclays Posts Higher Profit Despite One-Off Costs — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Elena Vardon 




  Barclays said profit edged higher as strong revenue growth was partially offset by a write-off linked to the collapse of U.K. mortgage finance company Market Financial Solutions. 



  The British bank on Tuesday posted a pretax profit of 2.81 billion pounds ($3.80 billion) for the first quarter, which was 3% higher than a year prior but missed consensus expectations slightly. 



  Group revenue rose 6% to 8.16 billion pounds, coming in a touch ahead of analyst forecasts. The increase was partly driven by higher net interest income--what it earns on loans minus what it pays out on client deposits--thanks to loan growth and hedging mechanisms designed to mitigate the impact of interest-rate moves. 



  Its top line was also supported by higher fees from its global markets and investment-banking operations within its largest division. While the conflict in the Middle East triggered late-quarter volatility that boosted equities trading, the gains lagged the lofty expectations set by the trading windfalls recently reported by major U.S. banks. Chief Executive C. S. Venkatakrishnan told reporters that unlike its Wall Street rivals, Barclays doesn't have a commodities business to capture the upside of the recent energy-market volatility and that its performance landed in the middle of the pack. 



  The executive described the quarterly performance as solid despite a one-off charge and impairments. 



  Barclays set aside more money than a year prior to cover bad loans, booking 823 million pounds in the quarter. This includes a 228 million-pound charge in the investment bank linked to its exposure to Market Financial Solutions, a company specializing in short-term loans for quick property purchases, which entered into administration earlier this year. 



  Barclays previously lent to MFS investment vehicles, which in turn made loans to mortgage borrowers. Venkatakrishnan noted last month that the financial impact would be materially lower than the roughly 500 million pounds MFS owed the bank, downplaying broader market jitters regarding recent turbulence in the private-credit space. 



  "We are constraining lending to certain structured finance counterparties who operate more vulnerable business models and cannot convince us of the quality and independence of their financial controls," Venkatakrishnan said. "These entities neither represent a material exposure nor a material source of foregone income, but their risk far outweighs any reward." 



  Given increased macroeconomic uncertainty, the bank is also reducing its exposure to potentially vulnerable, highly leveraged corporate borrowers. Elsewhere, Barclays said it is seeing stable payment rates and rational borrower behavior from clients across its U.K. and U.S. consumer and corporate businesses, and is monitoring rising energy costs and inflation for potential hits to consumer spending and economic growth. 



  Separately, the lender also increased its provision for a longrunning U.K. regulatory probe into car-loan commission practices and a related redress plan by 105 million pounds. The group stopped providing this kind of financing in 2019 and has set aside 430 million pounds to cover the fallout. 



  Shares fell as much as 4% in early trading in London after what some analysts described as mixed divisional trends and pointed to the unexpected car-loan redress top-up. 



  The bank stuck with its guidance for the year and the period through 2028. Barclays recently set out new targets building on a turnaround plan laid out two years ago to revitalize the group by cutting costs, shrinking the share of its capital-consuming investment bank, and growing steadier businesses such as its domestic retail bank. 



  Barclays intends to kick off an up to 500 million-pound buyback once its current 1 billion-pound program completes. 





  Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 28, 2026 05:53 ET (09:53 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: After report of OpenAI missing targets, one company sees its worst share-price decline in six months
data.description:
By Steve Goldstein

Oracle, AMD and CoreWeave slump in premarket trade

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman (L) and SoftBank group Chairman and CEO Masayoshi Son shake hands during a talk session in Tokyo on February 3, 2025. SoftBank shares slumped on Tuesday.

SoftBank Group shares tumbled in Tokyo trade on Tuesday, suffering its worst single-day percentage loss in six months, after a published report that OpenAI missed several internal targets in the face of competition from rival artificial-intelligence labs.

SoftBank stock (JP:9984) closed 9.9% lower in Tokyo trade, its worst day since Nov. 25. and the worst-performing component of the Nikkei 225 JP:NIK, after the Wall Street Journal reported that ChatGPT maker OpenAI missed several goals - for active users, yearly revenue and monthly revenue.

The report, citing people familiar with the matter, said Google's Gemini has eaten OpenAI market share while Anthropic has gained ground in coding and enterprise markets.

SoftBank is one of the companies that the hedge fund Coatue puts in the "OpenAI complex" that also includes Nvidia (NVDA), Oracle (ORCL), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Microsoft and CoreWeave (CRWV) due to their business relationships with the ChatGPT maker.

In premarket trade, Nvidia stock fell 1%, Oracle lost 5%, AMD dropped 4% and CoreWeave lost 5%.

Microsoft (MSFT), the largest shareholder in OpenAI, on Monday scaled back an OpenAI partnership, as a high-stakes trial between Elon Musk and OpenAI opened in San Francisco. Microsoft was little changed in premarket trade on Tuesday.

SoftBank has an 11% stake in OpenAI and also has made separate investments in AI data centers, making it essentially the purest play on OpenAI's success or failure. Last week, Bloomberg reported that SoftBank was seeking a $10 billion loan secured by its OpenAI stake.

"As [OpenAI] inches toward a potential IPO, the company now faces a convergence of risks that could significantly complicate its path to public markets: missed internal targets, intensifying global competition, and a high-stakes lawsuit that could reshape its very structure," said Christophe Barraud, head of discretionary management and research at Lior Global Partners.

The decline for SoftBank also comes after a big share-price decline for mostly held microchip designer ARM (ARM) on Monday.

-Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-28-26 0541ET

data.title: A second China shock is hitting Europe. These stocks are most vulnerable, strategist says.
data.description:
By Jules Rimmer

As China redirects its exports from the U.S. to Europe, companies on the Continent feel the pressure

China significantly cut its exports to the U.S after Trump imposed tariffs in April 2025 and redirected many of them to the U.K. and Europe.

Since the U.S. imposed significantly higher tariffs on almost all its trading partners last April, the world's largest exporter, China, has reacted swiftly. Its companies have been exporting aggressively to the U.K. and Europe, creating stiff competition and placing margins under pressure.

China adjusted its export flows rapidly in 2025, reducing its share going to the U.S. from 12.5% in 2024 to just 8.1%. In terms of absolute value, though, this was $308 billion and 29.7% less. Meanwhile, its exports to the E.U. jumped 11% and to the U.K. by 24%. China was able to redirect its exports so swiftly by dropping its prices sharply.

In a note published Tuesday, Panmure Liberum's chief strategist, Joachim Klement, highlighted that China's high-tech exports in particular, have accelerated sharply by volume while the value has dropped by a third.

Klement identifies U.K. stocks Luceco (UK:LUCE), Croda (UK:CRDA) and ASOS (UK:ASC) as dangerously susceptible to margin erosion while in Europe he puts BASF (XE:BAS) , Air Liquide (FR:AI) and Infineon (XE:IFX) in the same camp.

That said, he thinks some of the assumptions about margin pressure on Brenntag (XE:BNR) and Mondi (UK:MNDI) are overly pessimistic.

China remains the only major exporter that still lowers its prices

The E.U. will react by imposing tariffs, as Klement and his co-author Francisca Reis point out, but the tariffs are narrowly focused on certain products, just as they were when they put up retaliatory trade barriers against the U.S. in 2018 when President Trump's first administration put tariffs in place. To protect jobs and industries, the E.U. has no real choice but to respond firmly.

The Panmure note emphasizes that it is high-tech goods and industrial inputs where China export volumes have risen while prices have fallen, often by more than 50%, Klement observes. For consumer goods like clothing and furniture where the price declines have been less pronounced, a price discount of 20% can still be hugely destructive as these businesses in Europe and the U.K. typically operate on much finer margins.

The sectors most vulnerable are computers and electrical equipment, chemicals, machinery and equipment, and textiles and apparel, he says.

-Jules Rimmer

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-28-26 0533ET

data.title: U.K. Population to Grow More Slowly on Fewer Migrants — Update
data.description:
By Paul Hannon 




  The U.K.'s population will grow more slowly over coming years than previously expected as fewer migrants move to the country, according to the official statistics agency, a development that would likely slow economic growth. 



  The Office for National Statistics Tuesday said it now expects the U.K.'s population to total 71 million in 2034, down from 72.2 million in its previous forecast. It estimated the population at 69.3 million people in mid-2024. 



  With the number of deaths exceeding the number of births, the ONS said population growth would be entirely driven by migration. The ONS lowered its projection for annual inflows to 230,000 from 340,000. 



  The ONS expects deaths to exceed births by 450,000 during the decade to 2034. It projects that 7.3 million people will move to the country from overseas, while 5.1 million people will leave. 



  Migration to the U.K. jumped in the years after the Covid-19 pandemic, but fell sharply last year. 



  The ONS usually projects future flows by taking the average of the previous decade, but has now modified that practice and excluded 2022 through 2024 as years of "unusually high levels of migration." 



  "This approach provides a long-term average for future migration, which is likely to be more representative of prevailing trends and is closer to expert views on the potential range within which we may see average long-term net international migration," it said. 



  The current and previous governments have tightened restrictions on who can live and work in the country, while many of the country's political parties have expressed more negative views of immigration. 



  The population projections produced by the ONS are used by a wide variety of government departments and agencies, including the Office for Budget Responsibility, which monitors government debt. 



  A smaller population usually translates into slower economic growth, and fewer taxpayers to help fund the debt. Some economists expect to see a sharper decline in immigration, reflecting tighter rules, greater hostility to migrants and weaker growth prospects. 



  Earlier this year, the National Institute of Economic and Social Research warned that U.K. economy would be smaller, its debts larger and taxes higher within 15 years if immigration were to end. 



  "Despite evidence of a substantial drop in net immigration, the government and key opposition parties continue to advocate reducing levels further," economists at Oxford Economics said in a note to clients. 





  Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 28, 2026 05:35 ET (09:35 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
